practiceMCQs.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the appointment and office of the Governor of a state?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 153 of the Indian Constitution states that there shall be a Governor for each state. (Note: The 7th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1956 facilitated the appointment of the same person as Governor for two or more states).

practiceMCQs.question: What is the maximum permissible delay period that a State Legislative Council can impose on an ordinary bill passed by the Legislative Assembly?
practiceMCQs.explanation: An ordinary bill can be delayed by the Legislative Council for a maximum period of 3 months in the first instance and 1 month upon being re-passed by the Assembly, totaling a maximum cumulative delay of 4 months.

practiceMCQs.question: Which committee was appointed in 1986 by the Rajiv Gandhi government and strongly recommended constitutional status for Panchayati Raj institutions?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The L.M. Singhvi Committee, appointed in 1986, was the first major committee to recommend constitutional recognition for Panchayati Raj institutions and the revitalization of Gram Sabhas.

practiceMCQs.question: Under which constitutional article is the State Finance Commission constituted to review the financial position of local bodies?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 243I mandates that the Governor of a state shall, within five years from the commencement of the 73rd Amendment Act and every five years thereafter, constitute a Finance Commission to review financial positions.

practiceMCQs.question: Which of the following authorities appoints the Advocate General of a state?
options: B) Chief Justice of the State High Court
D) Chief Minister of the State
practiceMCQs.explanation: Under Article 165, the Governor of each state appoints a person who is qualified to be appointed a judge of a High Court to be the Advocate General for the state.

practiceMCQs.question: What is the minimum age required to contest elections for a Village Panchayat or Municipal Corporation in India?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The minimum age requirement to contest elections for local self-government bodies (Panchayats and Municipalities) is 21 years, whereas it is 25 years for the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies.

practiceMCQs.question: Which amendment act added the Eleventh Schedule to the Indian Constitution, listing 29 functional items for Panchayats?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 added Part IX and the Eleventh Schedule to the Constitution, containing 29 functional items devolved to Panchayati Raj Institutions.

practiceMCQs.question: Who has the power to create or abolish a Legislative Council in a state under Article 169 of the Constitution?
options: A) The State Legislative Assembly alone
B) The Governor of the State
D) The President of India upon Supreme Court advice
practiceMCQs.explanation: Under Article 169, Parliament can create or abolish a Legislative Council if the State Legislative Assembly passes a resolution to that effect by a special majority.

practiceMCQs.question: Which of the following functions can the Governor perform independently without the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers?
options: B) Reserving a bill for the consideration of the President
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: B) Reserving a bill for the consideration of the President
practiceMCQs.explanation: Reserving a bill for the consideration of the President under Article 200 is a key constitutional discretionary power of the Governor, whereas ordinances, pardons, and appointments are done on ministerial aid and advice.

practiceMCQs.question: How many functional items are listed in the Twelfth Schedule of the Constitution for Urban Local Bodies?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The Twelfth Schedule, added by the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act (1992), contains 18 functional items entrusted to Municipalities for urban planning and development.

practiceMCQs.question: Which committee first recommended a three-tier system of Panchayati Raj in India?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) recommended the establishment of a three-tier Panchayati Raj system: Gram Panchayat at the village level, Panchayat Samiti at the block level, and Zilla Parishad at the district level.

practiceMCQs.question: What is the maximum strength of a State Legislative Assembly as stipulated by the Constitution of India?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 170 specifies that a State Legislative Assembly shall consist of not more than 500 and not less than 60 members chosen by direct election from territorial constituencies in the state (with minor exceptions for smaller states like Sikkim or Goa).

practiceMCQs.question: Which constitutional article details the pardoning powers of the Governor of a state?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 161 grants the Governor the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, or remissions of punishment, or to suspend, remit, or commute the sentence of any person convicted against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the state extends.

practiceMCQs.question: Who conducts elections to the Panchayats and Municipalities within a state?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Under Articles 243K and 243ZA, the superintendence, direction, and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of all elections to the Panchayats and Municipalities are vested in the State Election Commission.

practiceMCQs.question: Which of the following describes the fundamental distinction between a Gram Sabha and a Gram Panchayat?
options: A) Gram Sabha is executive; Gram Panchayat is legislative.
B) Gram Sabha consists of all registered voters in a village; Gram Panchayat is an elected executive body.
C) Gram Sabha operates at the district level; Gram Panchayat operates at the village level.
D) Gram Sabha is appointed by the Governor; Gram Panchayat is elected by the block committee.
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: B) Gram Sabha consists of all registered voters in a village; Gram Panchayat is an elected executive body.
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 243A defines the Gram Sabha as a body consisting of persons registered in the electoral rolls relating to a village, representing direct democracy, whereas the Gram Panchayat is the smaller elected executive body.

practiceMCQs.question: Which committee famously characterized rural local bodies in 1985 as 'grass without roots' due to bureaucratic dominance and lack of regular elections?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The G.V.K. Rao Committee (1985) reviewed rural development programs and concluded that grassroots democratic bodies had become 'grass without roots' because elections were frequently delayed and bureaucracy overshadowed local representation.

practiceMCQs.question: In the case of a bicameral state legislature, where can a Money Bill be introduced?
options: A) In either the Legislative Assembly or Legislative Council
B) Only in the Legislative Council
C) Only in the Legislative Assembly
D) Joint sitting of both houses
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: C) Only in the Legislative Assembly
practiceMCQs.explanation: Similar to the Lok Sabha at the center, a Money Bill in a state can only be introduced in the Legislative Assembly on the recommendation of the Governor.

practiceMCQs.question: What is the tenure of a member of the State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad)?
options: C) 6 years, with one-third retiring every two years
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: C) 6 years, with one-third retiring every two years
practiceMCQs.explanation: The Legislative Council is a permanent house not subject to dissolution. One-third of its members retire every second year, and each member serves a term of 6 years.

practiceMCQs.question: Which article empowers the Governor to promulgate ordinances during the recess of the state legislature?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 213 empowers the Governor to promulgate ordinances when the state legislature is not in session, having the same force and effect as an act of the state legislature.

practiceMCQs.question: What proportion of total seats is constitutionally mandated to be reserved for women in Panchayati Raj Institutions under Article 243D?
options: D) Determined entirely at the discretion of the Parliament
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 243D mandates that not less than one-third of the total number of seats to be filled by direct election in every panchayat shall be reserved for women.

practiceMCQs.question: Which commission or committee examined Centre-State relations in the 1980s and recommended strict guidelines for the appointment and role of Governors?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Formed in 1983 under Justice R.S. Sarkaria, the Sarkaria Commission thoroughly examined Centre-State relations and proposed vital recommendations regarding the partisan use of Article 356 and Governor appointments.

practiceMCQs.question: What is the minimum age required to be appointed as the Governor of an Indian state?
practiceMCQs.explanation: According to Article 157, a person must be a citizen of India and have completed the age of 35 years to be eligible for appointment as Governor.

practiceMCQs.question: Which of the following bodies is classified as an urban local government under the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act?
practiceMCQs.explanation: The 74th Amendment Act provides for three types of urban local bodies: Nagar Panchayats (for transitional rural-urban areas), Municipal Councils (for smaller urban areas), and Municipal Corporations (for larger urban areas).

practiceMCQs.question: Can the Governor of a state pardon a death sentence or commute a court-martial sentence?
options: A) Yes, exactly like the President under Article 72.
B) Yes, but only with the written concurrence of the Supreme Court.
C) No, the Governor cannot pardon death sentences or court-martial punishments.
D) Yes, if the state cabinet passes a unanimous resolution.
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: C) No, the Governor cannot pardon death sentences or court-martial punishments.
practiceMCQs.explanation: Under Article 161, while the Governor has extensive pardoning powers, they cannot pardon a death sentence (which is exclusive to the President under Article 72) nor can they pardon sentences by court-martial.

practiceMCQs.question: Which of the following states currently has a bicameral legislature (Legislative Assembly and Legislative Council)?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Uttar Pradesh is one of the select states in India with a bicameral legislature, alongside Bihar, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana.

practiceMCQs.question: What is the primary role of a District Planning Committee (DPC) constituted under the 74th Amendment Act?
options: A) To conduct state assembly elections
B) To consolidate plans prepared by panchayats and municipalities in the district
C) To audit state tax collections
D) To manage state highway construction
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: B) To consolidate plans prepared by panchayats and municipalities in the district
practiceMCQs.explanation: Article 243ZD mandates the constitution of a District Planning Committee at the district level to consolidate plans prepared by the panchayats and municipalities and prepare a draft development plan for the district as a whole.

practiceMCQs.question: Who administers the oath of office to the Chief Minister of a state?
options: B) The Chief Justice of India
C) The Governor of the State
D) The Speaker of the State Legislative Assembly
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: C) The Governor of the State
practiceMCQs.explanation: Under Article 164, the Governor administers the oath of office and secrecy to the Chief Minister and other members of the state Council of Ministers.

practiceMCQs.question: Which committee recommended a two-tier system of Panchayati Raj consisting of Zilla Parishad and Mandal Panchayat in 1978?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Appointed by the Janata government, the Ashok Mehta Committee (1978) recommended replacing the traditional three-tier system with a two-tier system: Zilla Parishad at the district level and Mandal Panchayat at the grassroots.

practiceMCQs.question: What happens if a state legislature passes a bill and the Governor reserves it for the consideration of the President?
options: A) The bill automatically becomes law after 6 months.
B) The President has absolute veto power over it even if re-passed by the state legislature.
C) The bill lapses if the State Assembly is dissolved.
D) The Supreme Court must review its constitutional validity before the President acts.
practiceMCQs.correctAnswer: B) The President has absolute veto power over it even if re-passed by the state legislature.
practiceMCQs.explanation: When a bill is reserved by the Governor for the President (Article 201), the President is not bound to give assent even if the state legislature re-passes the bill, giving the President a form of absolute veto on state legislation.

practiceMCQs.question: Which part of the Indian Constitution contains provisions regarding Municipalities (Urban Local Government)?
practiceMCQs.explanation: Part IX-A of the Indian Constitution, comprising Articles 243P to 243ZG, was added by the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 to govern Urban Local Governments.
